notes.note: The Syrian port of Antioch on the Orontes, founded by Seleucus I; the time it took a traveller to reach Brundisium from there (twenty-eight days) gauges how slowly news of Caesar in the East reached Italy.

C. Trebonius, a Caesarian officer (and later one of Caesar's assassins), had been with the army in the East; his freedman is the source of Cicero's news that the nephew had won Caesar's pardon at Antioch.

A bitter quip: besides his brother Quintus and the nephew Quintus who denounced him, Cicero fears "other Quintuses" — that is, fresh betrayals from his own kin. Both Quintuses had laid the blame for the family's Pompeian course on Cicero to ingratiate themselves with Caesar.
A Caesarian agent charged with ferrying legions to Sicily for the coming African campaign; the patronymic "son of Quintus" distinguishes him in the formal Roman manner.

notes.anchor: to convey legions across into Sicily
notes.note: The legions were being staged in Sicily as the springboard for Caesar's invasion of Africa, where the Pompeian survivors under Scipio and Cato had massed their forces.
